question: A school bus ahead stops with its red lights flashing. You must:
choices: Slow to 10 mph and pass carefully
Make a complete stop no matter where your vehicle is positioned — same lane, opposing lane, or at an intersection — until the red lights are turned off
Stop only if you are behind the bus
Pass on the left if no children are visible
explanation: When a school bus's red lights flash, ALL traffic must make a complete stop and never pass in any direction until the bus finishes loading/unloading and moves on with its lights off.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 12 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: You are NOT required to stop for a stopped school bus only when:
choices: You are running late
The bus is in the opposite lanes of a road separated by a median 20 feet or more wide
No children are visible outside the bus
You are at an intersection
explanation: The only exception: the bus is traveling the opposite direction on a roadway separated by a median at least 20 feet wide. Everyone else — including traffic at intersections — must stop completely.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 12 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: Why should you avoid lingering beside a large truck on the highway?
choices: Trucks have large blind-spot 'No-Zones' — up to 20 feet in front, along both sides, and up to 200 feet behind
It is illegal to drive next to a truck
Trucks always drift between lanes
Truck drivers can't use mirrors
explanation: If you can't see the truck driver in the truck's side mirror, the driver can't see you. Pass completely and on the left, and never cut in close — a loaded truck at 55 mph needs over 335 feet to stop.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 64-66 — Tractor-Trailer No-Zone

question: You are overtaking a bicyclist on a two-lane road and an oncoming car is approaching. You should:
choices: Squeeze between the bicycle and the oncoming car
Honk so the cyclist moves onto the shoulder
Slow down, let the oncoming vehicle pass, THEN pass the bicycle with plenty of room
Follow the cyclist until they leave the road
explanation: Never pass between a bicyclist and oncoming traffic. Take hazards one at a time: let the oncoming car pass first, then give the cyclist plenty of room. Never pass where the street is too narrow.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Sharing the Road with Bicycles

question: A police car is stopped on the shoulder ahead with its lights flashing. Arkansas's Move Over law requires you to:
choices: Stop behind it
Move to the farthest lane away from it whenever possible, or slow down if you can't change lanes
Continue at the speed limit in your lane
Turn on your hazard lights and pass quickly
explanation: On a multilane highway you must move over to the farthest lane away from a stopped law-enforcement or emergency vehicle whenever possible. Violations carry fines up to $1,000, possible jail, and up to a 6-month license suspension.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 11 — Move Over Law

question: Motorcycles are entitled to:
choices: Half a lane of traffic
A full lane width, just like a car
The shoulder only
The right third of the lane
explanation: Never share a lane with a motorcycle — it needs the full lane width to maneuver. Allow 4 to 6 seconds of following distance behind a motorcycle, and look twice for them before turning, especially left turns.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 70 — Respect a Motorcycle

question: At a railroad crossing, the gates begin to come down just as you start to cross. You should:
choices: Stop immediately on the tracks
Back up quickly
Keep going — even if it means you will break the gate
Honk and wait
explanation: Never stop on the tracks. If gates start lowering while you're crossing, keep going even if you break the gate. Never drive around lowered gates, and remember a second train may come on another track.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 29 — Gates and Lights

question: The most common car-vs-motorcycle crash happens when:
choices: A motorcycle rear-ends a car
A car driver makes a LEFT TURN in front of an oncoming motorcycle
A motorcycle passes on the shoulder
A car backs into a parked motorcycle
explanation: Nearly 60% of motorcycle intersection crashes are caused by another vehicle turning left in front of the rider. Motorcycles are small and their speed is hard to judge — always take a second look before turning left.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 69 — Situations When Crashes Are Most Likely
